items.q: Why did Chartism fail in the short term?
items.correct: Mass petitions were rejected by Parliament; the movement split between moral-force and physical-force wings; economic improvement after 1848 reduced support.
items.misconception: Chartism failed because no one supported it.
items.random_wrong: Chartism failed because Queen Victoria personally banned the movement.
items.why: Chartism had millions of signatures and was hugely supported. It failed politically, not for lack of support — and five of its six points were eventually adopted.

items.q: Why did the factory system replace the domestic system?
items.correct: Water-powered then steam-powered machinery was too large and expensive for cottages; factories concentrated workers around machines, enabling discipline, supervision, and scale.
items.misconception: Factories appeared because steam engines were invented.
items.random_wrong: Factories appeared because the government banned home weaving.
items.why: The shift was about machinery scale and capital investment, not just invention. Domestic spinning continued; factories triumphed where mechanisation outscaled hand work.

items.q: Why did Lenin introduce the NEP in 1921?
items.correct: War Communism had collapsed the economy and provoked the Kronstadt revolt; the NEP allowed limited private trade to restore production and stabilise Bolshevik rule.
items.misconception: The NEP was Lenin's plan to permanently switch Russia to capitalism.
items.random_wrong: The NEP was introduced by Stalin to fund the Five-Year Plans.
items.why: Lenin called the NEP a strategic retreat, not a destination. Students miss the tension between socialist ideology and pragmatic recovery — central to understanding 1920s USSR.

items.q: Which 1938 conference ceded the Sudetenland to Germany?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Munich. “Locarno” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Who signed the 1939 non-aggression pact for the USSR?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Molotov. “Litvinov” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In what month and year did Hitler remilitarise the Rhineland?
items.why: Exact answer: 1936 AD. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
